chunk_summary: This section discusses jejunal feeding as an escalation option for persistent reflux-related aspiration or vomiting, alongside guidelines for gastrostomy decision-making and peri-procedure risk mitigation in children with neurodisability.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications for jejunal feeding in children with neurodisability.
Review guidelines for gastrostomy tube placement and replacement protocols.
Identify strategies to mitigate peri-procedure risks in gastrostomy interventions.
Explore respiratory health considerations in cerebral palsy during feeding support.
key_facts: Jejunal feeding is recommended when reflux-related aspiration or vomiting persists despite optimization of care.
The ESPGHAN Position Paper (2021) provides detailed guidelines for percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy in children.
ASPEN Pediatric G-tube Guidelines include protocols for balloon gastrostomy replacement.
Respiratory health in cerebral palsy is a critical factor in gastrostomy decision-making, as outlined by AACPDM Care Pathways.
Canadian Paediatric Society emphasizes the importance of individualized gastrostomy tube feeding plans for children and youth.
reference_quote: Jejunal feeding is an escalation option when reflux-related aspiration or vomiting persists despite optimization. (ESPGHAN Position Paper, 2021)
document_key_topics: Jejunal feeding for persistent reflux-related aspiration
Gastrostomy tube placement and replacement protocols
Respiratory health in cerebral palsy during feeding interventions
